title: Alaska Day: America's Biggest Purchase Party
story: Every October 18, Alaska celebrates the day it became part of America! In 1867, the United States bought Alaska from Russia for $7.2 million. People in Alaska mark this special day with parades, festivals, and flag ceremonies. Schools often teach kids about Alaska's history, and communities host special events to honor their state's unique journey to becoming American.
activity_question: Who did the United States buy Alaska from in 1867?
bonus_fact: Alaska was nicknamed "Seward's Folly" because many thought it was a waste of money!

title: Sweet Treats Take the Spotlight!
story: Chocolate cupcakes rule the world on October 18 during National Chocolate Cupcake Day! Bakeries across the country whip up extra batches of these mini chocolate cakes topped with creamy frosting. Families bake together in their kitchens, filling homes with the smell of cocoa and vanilla. Schools sometimes serve chocolate cupcakes at lunch, and coworkers bring them to share at the office. Social media fills with photos of perfectly swirled frosting and sprinkle-covered masterpieces. Some people try crazy flavor combinations like chocolate-peanut butter or chocolate-strawberry. Whether you buy them from a fancy bakery or make them from a box mix, these little cakes bring big smiles on this sweet holiday!
activity_question: What do families often do together on National Chocolate Cupcake Day?
bonus_fact: The first cupcakes were baked in small cups before muffin tins were invented!
